one_line_takeaway: Per-instance privacy losses, borrowed from per-instance differential privacy, give a theoretically grounded, empirically validated predictor of how hard individual data points are to unlearn via fine-tuning, out-performing prior heuristic difficulty proxies at flagging the truly hard-to-forget examples.
key_finding: Across SGLD, SGD, and L1-Sparse unlearning, forget sets with higher average per-instance privacy loss consistently require more fine-tuning steps to unlearn (validated on all three metrics: UA, MIA, GUS), confirming the theoretical prediction. Privacy losses correlate strongly with all five proxy difficulty metrics (average gradient norm is the best but most expensive proxy), yet when selecting the top-s hardest points (s in {600, 1000, 2000}), the sets picked by privacy losses are strictly harder to unlearn than those picked by C-Proxy, average gradient norm, or EL2N. Geometrically, higher-privacy-loss forget sets start with larger loss barriers to the oracle, and after fine-tuning the loss barrier drops to the baseline level between two independently trained oracles, evidencing successful unlearning under this stringent test.
core_contribution: The paper introduces per-instance privacy losses as a principled, data-point-level measure of how hard a training example is to unlearn via (noisy) fine-tuning. It ports the per-instance Renyi-DP analysis of Thudi et al. (2024) into the unlearning setting and sharpens the Langevin-unlearning convergence analysis of Chien et al. (2024): the paper's OWN theoretical results (Corollary 4.1, Definition 4.2, Theorem 4.3, Corollary 4.4) replace a worst-case Renyi-DP bound with per-instance privacy losses P(x, alpha), and show that the number k of noisy-gradient-descent steps needed to (alpha, delta)-unlearn a single point x grows only logarithmically in its per-instance privacy loss (k >= A_alpha ln[(B_alpha P(x,4alpha) + C_alpha eps_{4alpha-1})/(delta - eps_{2alpha-1})]). Empirically, the paper shows these privacy losses predict unlearning difficulty not just for the noisy SGLD setting matching the theory, but also for noiseless SGD and for the L1-Sparse SOTA unlearning method. Secondary contributions: privacy losses correlate strongly with cheaper proxy difficulty metrics yet identify strictly harder-to-unlearn forget sets than those proxies, and a new loss-barrier (linear-mode-connectivity) evaluation of unlearning efficacy.
threat_model: Machine unlearning is motivated by data-deletion requests (GDPR 'right to be forgotten'), mitigating data-poisoning, and removing stale data. The privacy/security concern the paper works within is distinguishability: an adversary who can tell that a purportedly deleted point still influences the model. The paper's guarantees bound the alpha-Renyi divergence D_alpha(mu || nu) between the unlearned model distribution and the retrain-from-scratch (oracle) distribution, so a small bound means the deletion is statistically indistinguishable from never having trained on the point. Efficacy is stress-tested against three adversary-style probes: a membership-inference attack (logistic-regression MIA), and the Gaussian Unlearning Score (GUS) which uses Gaussian input-poisoning to check whether the unlearned model still encodes poison patterns tied to the forget set. The paper does not propose a new attack or defend a specific deployed system; it is a measurement/analysis contribution to privacy-preserving unlearning.
limitations: Theoretical guarantees strictly hold only for SGLD (noisy gradient descent) under Lipschitz/smoothness assumptions; the extension to standard SGD relies on an unjustified assumed implicit noise (sigma <= 0.1) and is empirical only, with the authors flagging that a theoretical justification via hardware/software nondeterminism is an open problem. The group-unlearning analysis is explicitly acknowledged as not tight: implementing Thudi et al.'s group privacy accounting gave bounds too loose to differentiate forget sets that empirically differ in unlearning cost (Appendix Figure 4), and obtaining a tight group bound is left open. The Corollary 4.1 bound has an irreducible eps_{2alpha-1} term (distance to stationarity) that does not vanish with more unlearning steps. Empirical scope is limited to two vision datasets (SVHN, CIFAR-10) with ResNet-18 (ViT-small only in appendix); no NLP/LLM experiments despite unlearning's relevance there. Privacy-loss computation still requires storing per-step gradients, motivating the proxy study.

quantitative_results: Steps to unlearn a single point x scale logarithmically in its per-instance privacy loss: k >= A_alpha ln[(B_alpha P(x,4alpha) + C_alpha eps_{4alpha-1})/(delta - eps_{2alpha-1})] (Corollary 4.4)
Per-instance privacy loss bounds the retrain divergence: D_alpha(nu_{T,D} || nu_{T,D'}) <= P(x, alpha) for D' = D \ {x} (Theorem 4.3)
Privacy loss estimated from N=35 evenly spaced checkpoints, ~20% of training time, versus 150 epochs (full-trajectory) needed for C-Proxy and average gradient norm
5 forget sets of 1000 data points each, spanning difficulty by average privacy loss
Top-s hardest forget sets evaluated at sizes s in {600, 1000, 2000}; privacy-loss-selected sets take longest to unlearn
Time-to-unlearn defined as epochs to reach within 5% of the oracle model's metric value (UA or MIA)
For noiseless SGD, implicit noise assumed sigma <= 0.1 (rankings stable to this choice)
Post-unlearning loss barrier drops to the baseline barrier measured between two independently trained oracle models
baselines_compared: C-Proxy memorization proxy (Zhao et al. 2024, adapted from Feldman et al. 2018 / Jiang et al. 2020)
Average gradient norm across all training iterations
Gradient norm at a single mid-training checkpoint
Gradient norm at end of training
L1-Sparse fine-tuning (Liu et al. 2024a) as the SOTA unlearning algorithm the privacy-loss predictions are also tested on
Oracle: model retrained from scratch on the retain set only (gold-standard unlearning target)
